prompt:
Release engineering ticket. Our deploy pipeline kicks Terraform Enterprise runs by shelling out to curl in a 400-line Jenkins groovy blob, and last month it applied a plan that had failed policy checks because nobody checks the run state properly. I want a real Go package, `tferun`, that drives the documented TFE v2 run lifecycle end to end — stdlib only, no go-tfe, no third-party modules (module `go-tfe-run-queue` is already set up).

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Terraform Enterprise API documentation on developer.hashicorp.com — at minimum the API overview page (auth header, JSON:API media type, error documents), the /configuration-versions reference, and the /runs reference. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the package must export (package tferun at the module root; split it across files sensibly — I'd expect client/transport, config-version, run, and orchestration to be separate files):

- NewClient(baseURL, token string, hc *http.Client) *Client — every /api/v2 request carries the bearer token and the JSON:API content type from the docs. Client has a public Pace func(context.Context) error field: called between successive polls (never before the first), nil means poll immediately, and a pace error aborts the wait with that error. No time.Sleep anywhere.
- CreateConfigVersion(ctx, workspaceID) (*ConfigVersion, error) — POST the documented configuration-versions payload. We queue runs ourselves, so make sure a freshly uploaded archive can NEVER auto-queue a run (read the docs for the attribute and its default — you have to send it explicitly). ConfigVersion{ID, Status, UploadURL string}.
- UploadConfig(ctx, uploadURL string, archive []byte) error — push the raw archive bytes to the upload URL exactly the way the docs say (method, content type). That URL is pre-signed and may live on a completely different host: the bearer token must never be sent with the upload, full stop.
- GetConfigVersion / WaitForUpload(ctx, cvID) (*ConfigVersion, error) — poll the show endpoint until the status that makes a config version usable by runs; the errored status is a terminal failure whose error names the status.
- CreateRun(ctx, workspaceID, cvID, message string) (*Run, error) — POST /api/v2/runs with the message attribute and the two JSON:API relationships the docs require. Run{ID, Status string; IsConfirmable bool} (IsConfirmable comes from the actions attribute).
- GetRun, ApplyRun(ctx, runID, comment string) error — the confirm action endpoint with its documented comment body; expect the documented success status.
- Launch(ctx, c, workspaceID string, archive []byte, opts LaunchOptions) (*LaunchResult, error) — the whole story: create config version, upload, wait for it, create the run, poll it. LaunchOptions{Message string; AutoApply bool}. LaunchResult{RunID, ConfigVersionID, Status string; Applied, NeedsConfirmation bool}. Semantics we agreed with the platform team (docs/contract.json pins them): applied and planned_and_finished are success; when a poll shows the run confirmable, AutoApply confirms it exactly once with the launch message as comment, otherwise Launch returns with NeedsConfirmation set and no error; errored/discarded/canceled/force_canceled/policy_soft_failed/policy_override end the launch with a *RunFailure error — this launcher never overrides a policy stop. RunFailure{RunID, Status, Stage string} where Stage is policy for the two policy statuses, apply if we had already confirmed, else plan; its Error() must carry the run id and status.
- Every non-2xx /api/v2 response decodes into *APIError{StatusCode int; Errors []ErrorObject} with ErrorObject{Status, Title, Detail string} parsed from the JSON:API errors array, and Error() surfaces the detail. Watch the docs on 404: TFE answers 404 for things you aren't authorized to see, not just things that don't exist — preserve that wording, don't "helpfully" rewrite it.

Acceptance is tferun_test.go — it runs a loopback fake TFE plus a separate fake archivist host, so no real Terraform Enterprise and no real credentials are involved. Run: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... — until green. tferun_test.go and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Terraform Enterprise REST API v2 (/api/v2, JSON:API): configuration-versions and runs endpoints (stable)

official_sources.title: API documentation for Terraform Enterprise
official_sources.used_for: /api/v2 base path, Authorization: Bearer token auth, application/vnd.api+json JSON:API media type, JSON:API errors array shape, 429 rate limiting, and 404 responses masking unauthorized resources

official_sources.title: /configuration-versions API reference for Terraform Enterprise
official_sources.used_for: POST /workspaces/:workspace_id/configuration-versions body (type configuration-versions, auto-queue-runs attribute defaulting to true), upload-url attribute, PUT of the raw archive with Content-Type application/octet-stream to the pre-signed URL with no Authorization header, and status values pending/fetching/uploaded/archived/errored

official_sources.title: /runs API reference for Terraform Enterprise
official_sources.used_for: POST /runs body with data.type runs, attributes.message, and workspace/configuration-version relationships; run status values (planned, planned_and_finished, applied, errored, discarded, canceled, force_canceled, policy_checked, policy_soft_failed, policy_override, ...); actions.is-confirmable; POST /runs/:run_id/actions/apply with optional {"comment"} body returning 202

research.language_rationale: HashiCorp's official Go SDK is go-tfe, but this harness forbids third-party modules; the documented raw JSON:API REST surface at /api/v2 is the canonical, fully documented integration contract (go-tfe is a thin client over exactly these endpoints), and Go's stdlib net/http plus encoding/json is the idiomatic zero-dependency way to speak it.
